# Autopoiesis

## Explains

Explains why self-maintaining systems are more resilient than externally-maintained ones.

## The Concept

A system that maintains and improves itself through its own operation is autopoietic — it produces the conditions for its own continuation. Rather than depending on an external maintainer to keep it healthy, the system's normal functioning generates the maintenance, repair, and renewal it requires. The governance system creates and destroys its own components as part of routine operation, not as exceptional intervention.

Slot dynamics (birth/kill) are autopoietic — the system creates and destroys its own components. When a governance object becomes obsolete, it is retired through a process that is itself part of the governance system. When a new need emerges, the system has mechanisms to spawn new objects. This self-regulation is not accidental; it is designed into the structure.

The biological analogy is precise and instructive. Apoptosis (programmed cell death) is healthy maintenance — cells that cannot die become cancerous. Competence windows define when a component is viable. Morphogenesis requires death: new structures emerge only when old ones are cleared. The mappings to governance are direct: apoptosis maps to planned slot retirement, competence windows map to lifecycle gates, morphogen gradients map to authority cascading, cell differentiation maps to template-to-instance specialization, and homeostasis maps to health model equilibrium.

Systems that depend on external maintenance are fragile because the maintainer becomes a single point of failure, operates on a different tempo than the system itself, and lacks the intimate knowledge that internal processes possess. Autopoietic governance removes this dependency.

## Why This Matters for Governance

A governance system that cannot maintain itself decays the moment its creator stops actively tending it. This is the fate of most corporate governance: policies written once, never updated, increasingly disconnected from reality. JOS avoids this by embedding self-maintenance into the system's operation — review cadences, lifecycle stages, health scores, and retirement processes are all part of the system maintaining itself.

The implication is that every governance object must carry its own maintenance metadata (when was it last reviewed? is it still needed? what is its health?) and the system must have processes that act on that metadata without requiring external intervention.

## Connects To

- JOS-C11 (Evolutionary Optimization) — evolution is the mechanism by which autopoietic systems improve
- JOS-C21 (System Entropy) — autopoiesis is the counter-force to entropy; without it, systems decay
- Framework slot dynamics §8-9 — the concrete implementation of autopoietic birth/kill cycles

## Provenance

Foundry Evolution concepts combined with GOLD #11 panel biology analogy. Original concept of self-maintaining systems from Maturana and Varela's biological autopoiesis theory, reframed as a governance architecture principle.

### Framework Connection

Framework §7 (template/instance self-maintenance via Empty/Null/Filled states), §8 (slot dynamics with birth/kill cycles), and §10 (health model equilibrium) provide the concrete autopoietic mechanisms — the system creates, monitors, and retires its own components as part of routine operation.
